summary: Jeff Atwood argues that syntax highlighting has become essential for reading code and markup, not just a convenience. He contrasts viewing XML in plain Notepad versus a colorizing editor, demonstrating how dramatically readability improves with color. The post questions whether developers have become spoiled or whether colorization genuinely aids comprehension. Atwood concludes that while plain text editors work fine for prose, they're inadequate for code.
key_points: Syntax highlighting transforms incomprehensible 'angle bracket noise' into readable, structured content
Plain text editors like Notepad are adequate for prose but fail for code and markup
The visual distinction provided by colorization helps developers parse and understand code structure
The dependency on colorization raises questions about whether it's a genuine cognitive aid or learned preference
money_quotes: I can't bear to read them in black and white any more.
It's just a slew of angle bracket noise that makes my brain hurt.
We need colorization to truly understand what we're looking at.
Why is that? Are we just spoiled?
key_insight: Syntax highlighting has evolved from a nice-to-have feature to a cognitive necessity for parsing the dense symbolic structures of code and markup.
